channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Code Evolved More in 60 Years than Writing did in 5,000—Now AI is About to Rewrite Writing
executive_summary: In the last 60 years, coding has evolved more rapidly than writing did in 5,000 years because code was designed for computers, while natural language only recently became compute-native with the advent of large language models (LLMs). This shift allows for the creation of AI-native writing tools that streamline the writing process, similar to how DevOps transformed software development. As a result, knowledge work could see productivity gains similar to the 200× improvements experienced in coding through continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD).
bullets: Code has evolved more in 60 years than writing did in 5,000 years because it was designed for computers, while natural language has only recently become compute-native. This matters because it highlights the potential for AI to revolutionize how we write, making it more efficient and integrated with technology. Think of it like a car that was built for the road versus a bicycle that can only navigate rough terrain.

The future of writing will adopt a DevOps-style pipeline, which includes stages like draft, test, stage, and deploy. This is significant because it allows writers to systematically improve their work with AI assistance at each step, similar to how software developers refine code. Imagine a factory assembly line where each worker specializes in a different part of the process, ensuring a polished final product.

Chatbots are currently transitional tools in the writing process, but the breakthrough will come with AI-native document tools that automate variant generation, fact verification, and multi-channel deployment. This is crucial because it will make complex writing tasks easier and faster, much like a Swiss Army knife that has multiple tools for different tasks at your fingertips.

Professional AI users are already chaining models like O3 for idea generation, Opus 4 for structuring, Perplexity for fact-checking, and Claude Sonnet for polishing their writing. This proves that the concept of a writing pipeline is viable and effective, akin to a chef using various specialized utensils to prepare a gourmet meal.

The potential for knowledge work to leverage AI-native pipelines could unlock the same 200× productivity gains that coding experienced with CI/CD. This is important because it means that tasks which involve document creation could become exponentially faster and more efficient. Think of it like a factory that can produce 200 times more widgets with the same amount of labor.

Voice interfaces are not the real innovation in writing; they merely revive pre-literate habits. The true novelty lies in compute-aligned writing workflows that can handle the complexity of language, much like how a modern smartphone does more than just make calls.

Nate B. Jones states, 'I’m standing on the edge of a different way of writing—one that treats text like deployable code.' This quote emphasizes the shift towards viewing writing as a process that can be optimized and automated, similar to how software is developed and deployed.

Natural language has been 'bolted onto' computers for decades, but LLMs finally make it a first-class, compute-native medium. This matters because it allows machines to understand human text in a way that was previously impossible, much like how a translator can finally bridge the gap between two languages.

The future platforms for writing will automate processes like drafting, verification, and deployment, making them as seamless as a CI/CD run. This is significant because it means that writers can focus on creativity rather than logistics, like a musician who can play without worrying about tuning their instrument.

The current manual pipelines used by professional AI users demonstrate that chaining models can effectively mimic the desired writing pipeline. This is crucial because it shows that the technology is already capable of supporting complex writing tasks, much like a relay race where each runner passes the baton smoothly to the next.

The shift towards AI-native writing tools will redefine how we approach document creation and could lead to a new era of productivity in knowledge work. This is important because it suggests that the traditional methods of writing may soon become obsolete, much like how typewriters were replaced by computers.

The integration of AI into writing processes will require a mindset shift among writers and professionals, who must adapt to new tools and workflows. This is essential because those who resist change may find themselves left behind, similar to how businesses that did not embrace the internet fell behind their competitors.
key_takeaways: Implement a DevOps-style pipeline for your writing process, including stages for drafting, testing, staging, and deploying. This matters because it will help you systematically improve your writing and make it more efficient. If you skip this, you may find your writing process chaotic and unproductive.

Start chaining AI models like O3, Opus 4, Perplexity, and Claude Sonnet in your writing workflow. This will allow you to leverage the strengths of each model, improving your writing quality and efficiency. If you don’t, you may miss out on the benefits of a streamlined writing process.

By the end of the year, aim to adopt at least one AI-native document tool that automates variant generation and fact verification. This is important because it will save you time and improve the accuracy of your writing. If you delay, you risk falling behind in a rapidly evolving field.

Set a goal to increase your productivity by 200% by integrating AI into your writing processes. This is crucial because it reflects the gains seen in coding with CI/CD, and it will position you ahead of your peers. If you don’t, you may struggle to keep up with the demands of modern knowledge work.

technical_terms.DevOps: A methodology that combines software development and IT operations to improve efficiency and speed in delivering applications. Think of it like a well-oiled machine where each part works together seamlessly to produce a final product.
technical_terms.CI/CD: Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment are practices that allow developers to frequently integrate code changes and deploy them automatically. Like a conveyor belt that moves products through various stages of production without stopping.
technical_terms.AI-native: Tools or processes designed specifically to leverage artificial intelligence capabilities from the ground up. Think of it like a smartphone built to take advantage of the internet, rather than just a phone with internet access.
technical_terms.Large Language Models (LLMs): Advanced AI systems that can understand and generate human language with high complexity. Like a highly skilled translator who not only knows the words but also understands the nuances and context of the conversation.
technical_terms.Model Chaining: The practice of linking multiple AI models together to enhance the output of a process. Think of it like a relay race where each runner passes the baton to the next, improving the overall speed and efficiency.
technical_terms.Variant Generation: The process of creating multiple versions of a document or text to explore different ideas or styles. Like a chef experimenting with different recipes to find the best flavor combination.
technical_terms.Fact Verification: The process of checking the accuracy of information presented in a document. Think of it like a detective gathering evidence to confirm the truth of a story.
technical_terms.Document Pipeline: A structured process for creating, editing, and deploying written content. Like an assembly line where each step is carefully designed to ensure quality and efficiency.
technical_terms.O3: An AI model used for generating ideas during the writing process. Think of it like a brainstorming partner who helps you come up with fresh concepts.
technical_terms.Opus 4: An AI model that assists in structuring written content. Like an architect who designs the layout of a building before construction begins.
technical_terms.Perplexity: An AI model used for fact-checking and verifying information. Think of it like a librarian who ensures that all the information in a book is accurate and credible.
technical_terms.Claude Sonnet: An AI model used for polishing and refining written content. Like a skilled editor who reviews a manuscript to enhance its clarity and flow.
